original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch Causes Progressive Pool Insolvency
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: All deposit functions (executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint) credit the full nominal amount to internal reserves rather than measuring the actual balance change. With fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than the nominal amount, causing reserves to systematically exceed actual balances. Over time, the pool becomes insolvent: later LPs and swappers cannot withdraw because the contract tries to transfer more tokens than it holds. Early participants who detect this can extract real value before the deficit becomes apparent.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Lines ~121 (executeFeeSwap: reserveUserToken += amountIn), ~148 (rebalanceSwap: reserveValidatorToken += amountIn), and ~200 (mint: reserveValidatorToken += amountValidatorToken) all use the nominal input amount, never checking actual received balance.
deployment_assumptions: Pool must include a fee-on-transfer token (e.g., STA, PAXG, deflationary tokens)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No token whitelist exists; contract is permissionless; no balance-before/after measurement in any function

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserve += nominal_amount without checking balanceOf before/after transfer

preconditions: Pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token as userToken or validatorToken
Multiple users interact with the pool over time, accumulating accounting deficit
No mechanism exists to reconcile reserves with actual balances

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Reserve accounting uses nominal transfer amounts, not actual received amounts
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: reserves > actual balanceOf after each fee-on-transfer deposit; deficit grows linearly with transaction count
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance-before/after measurement; no SafeERC20; no token type validation

analysis_questions: What fee-on-transfer tokens exist on the target deployment chain?
How quickly does the deficit grow to cause burn/swap reverts?
Can an early LP strategically exit before insolvency while others are trapped?
validation_requirements: Create pool with simulated 2% fee-on-transfer token
Execute multiple deposits and swaps, track reserve vs balance divergence
Verify last LP's burn reverts due to insufficient actual balance

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Pool created with fee-on-transfer token. Each deposit credits more to reserves than actually received. After many transactions, reserveToken exceeds actual balance by significant amount. Early LPs burn and receive their full calculated share (contract still has enough). Last LPs' burns revert as contract tries to transfer more than it holds. Early exiters profit at expense of later ones.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Last LPs lose entire deposited value; funds permanently locked in insolvent pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Later LPs who cannot withdraw due to insufficient contract balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism exists; trapped funds are unrecoverable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitorable by comparing contract balanceOf vs sum of pool reserves

validation_result.impact_summary: All deposit/swap paths credit nominal transfer amounts to internal reserves without measuring actual received balance, causing progressive insolvency with fee-on-transfer tokens that traps later users' funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis is correct. Every function that receives tokens into the contract (executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint) updates internal pool reserves using the nominal `amountIn` or `amountValidatorToken` parameter, never measuring `balanceOf(address(this))` before and after the transfer. With a fee-on-transfer token, the contract receives fewer tokens than requested, but reserves record the full amount. This creates a growing deficit between tracked reserves and actual balances. Over time, burn() and swap functions will attempt to transfer out more tokens than the contract holds, reverting and trapping user funds.

The contract has no token whitelist, no balance reconciliation mechanism, and pool creation is fully permissionless — any address pair can be used. There is no SafeERC20 usage and no before/after balance check pattern anywhere in the codebase.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment:
All three deposit paths exhibit the same pattern: transferFrom is called with a nominal amount, then the same nominal amount is added to reserves without verifying actual receipt. Specifically:

1. executeFeeSwap line ~119-121: transferFrom(amountIn) then reserveUserToken += amountIn
2. rebalanceSwap line ~148,153: reserveValidatorToken += amountIn then transferFrom(amountIn)
3. mint line ~198,203: transferFrom(amountValidatorToken) then reserveValidatorToken += amountValidatorToken

None of these paths include a balance check before and after the transfer to determine the actual amount received.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: If userToken is a fee-on-transfer token, the contract receives less than amountIn, but reserves are credited the full amountIn. The deficit accumulates with every swap.

If validatorToken is fee-on-transfer, reserves are inflated before the transfer even occurs (optimistic update). The state update uses the full amountIn but fewer tokens arrive, creating an immediate deficit.

LP deposits with a fee-on-transfer validatorToken credit the full amountValidatorToken to reserves but the contract receives less. LP tokens are minted as if the full amount was deposited. The pool starts insolvent from the first deposit.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls mint(userToken, validatorToken, amountValidatorToken, to) where validatorToken has 2% transfer fee
2. Pool is uninitialized: liquidity = amountValidatorToken/2 - MIN_LIQUIDITY is computed based on nominal amount
3. IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom transfers amountValidatorToken but contract receives only 98% (line ~198)
4. pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken) credits full 100% to reserves (line ~203)
5. totalSupply and liquidityBalances updated based on nominal amount (lines ~205-206)
7. Subsequent swaps and mints compound the deficit
8. Eventually burn() calculates amountValidatorToken = liquidity * reserveValidatorToken / totalSupply which exceeds actual balance
9. IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(to, amountValidatorToken) in burn reverts due to insufficient balance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Every token-receiving path (executeFeeSwap line 119, rebalanceSwap line 153, mint line 198) uses IERC20.transferFrom with a nominal amount, then credits the same nominal amount to pool.reserve* variables. No balanceOf check exists before or after any transfer in the entire contract. The burn function (line 239-240) then attempts to transfer the proportional share based on inflated reserves, which will eventually exceed the actual contract balance.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Pool must include a fee-on-transfer token (e.g., STA, PAXG, deflationary tokens)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Only foundry.toml is provided as a deployment configuration. It specifies Solidity 0.8.28 and OpenZeppelin imports but contains no token whitelist, no deployment scripts specifying which tokens will be used, and no restrictions on pool creation. The contract is permissionless — any user can call mint() with any token pair.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid and straightforward: 1) Pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token (e.g., 2% fee). 2) First LP deposits 100,000 tokens via mint(). Contract receives 98,000 but reserves show 100,000. Deficit: 2,000. 3) Multiple users swap via executeFeeSwap, each time crediting full amountIn to reserveUserToken but receiving less. 4) More LPs deposit, each time increasing the deficit. 5) Early LPs call burn() — the contract still has enough real tokens to honor their proportional share. 6) As the deficit compounds, later burns revert because the contract attempts to transfer more than its actual balance. 7) Last LPs' funds are permanently trapped. The attack requires no special permissions, flash loans, or sophisticated timing — just normal usage with fee-on-transfer tokens.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. If a pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token: (1) Progressive insolvency grows linearly with transaction count; (2) Later LPs and swappers cannot withdraw their funds; (3) Early participants who understand the deficit can exit before insolvency, extracting value at expense of later users; (4) No recovery mechanism exists — trapped funds are unrecoverable; (5) The permissionless pool creation means anyone can create such a pool, potentially as a griefing attack against unsuspecting LPs.
recommended_mitigations: Use balance-before/after pattern: measure IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)) before and after each transferFrom, and use the actual difference for reserve accounting
Use OpenZeppelin SafeERC20 (safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom) to handle non-standard return values
Consider adding a token whitelist or validation mechanism to prevent pools with non-standard token types
Add a reconciliation function or invariant check that verifies reserves <= actual balances
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC20 with 2% transfer fee. 2. Deploy FeeAMM. 3. Create pool via mint() with fee-on-transfer token as validatorToken (deposit 100,000 tokens). 4. Verify pool.reserveValidatorToken == 100,000 but balanceOf(FeeAMM) == 98,000. 5. Perform several executeFeeSwap operations to accumulate userToken reserves with same deficit pattern. 6. Have a second LP mint into the pool. 7. First LP calls burn() — succeeds but extracts disproportionate real value. 8. Second LP calls burn() — reverts because contract lacks sufficient balance. 9. Confirm funds are permanently trapped.
uncertainties: The exact fee-on-transfer tokens available on the target deployment chain are unknown
No deployment scripts specify which tokens are intended for use — the vulnerability depends on permissionless pool creation being used with such tokens
Some fee-on-transfer tokens may have very small fees, requiring many transactions before insolvency becomes critical
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with varying fee-on-transfer percentages (0.01% to 10%) to determine minimum transactions needed for insolvency
Test with actual fee-on-transfer tokens (STA, PAXG, USDT with fee enabled) on target chain
Test interaction between multiple pools sharing the same fee-on-transfer token to verify cross-pool insolvency impact
Test whether the deficit can be exploited by early exiters for profit extraction
